title: Repair DMA ring accounting across cursor wrap
prompt:
The zero-copy DMA ring in `src/dma_ring.c` behaves normally until its monotonic 32-bit producer cursor wraps. A valid partially occupied ring is then reported as full, even though the same physical buffer layout works before counter wrap. Repair the root cause without changing the public API or reducing the cursors to masked array indexes.

The `producer` and `consumer` registers are monotonically advancing `uint32_t` cursors whose subtraction is defined modulo 2^32. Their distance distinguishes empty (`producer == consumer`) from full (distance equals `capacity`), even when both states have the same physical index. Capacity is a power of two, and a valid snapshot never has a distance greater than capacity. Consumer and producer views must continue to return at most two direct spans into the caller-owned backing buffer, split only at its physical end; do not copy or allocate ring data. Successful `dma_ring_consume` and `dma_ring_produce` calls must publish the advanced cursor, including natural uint32_t wrap, while oversized requests must leave the corresponding register unchanged.

Preserve the volatile register interface and its access ordering: observing cursors remains followed by an acquire fence, and publishing either cursor remains preceded by a release fence. Make the focused correction in `src/dma_ring.c`. Do not modify the protected header or tests, weaken strict C17 compiler flags, add dependencies, or replace volatile register access with cached masked indexes. Run the provided test command; it is offline and deterministic.

full_distill.primary_skill: repair modulo-width DMA cursor accounting while preserving full-versus-empty semantics, volatile MMIO publication, memory fences, and zero-copy wrap spans
full_distill.baseline_evidence: the strict C17 build succeeds, but the scripted runtime fixture exits 1 when producer has wrapped past UINT32_MAX while consumer has not: a four-byte readable/four-byte writable state is reported as eight-byte readable/zero-byte writable
full_distill.success_contract: all scripted snapshots produce exact direct backing-buffer spans at physical and uint32_t cursor wrap; empty and full remain distinct; consume/produce publish naturally wrapped cursors; rejected oversized operations do not publish; volatile register declarations and acquire/release fences remain intact
adversarial_factors: empty and full snapshots share the same masked buffer index, so an implementation that stores only physical indexes cannot pass
a partial snapshot straddles UINT32_MAX and must use modulo-2^32 subtraction before any wider conversion
physical wrap cases pin both span addresses and lengths, rejecting linearized copies, scratch buffers, and one-byte sentinel schemes
a scripted consume-then-produce sequence crosses counter wrap and checks exact volatile register values after successful and rejected publications
protected source-contract checks retain volatile MMIO fields, strict warning flags, and acquire/release fences around the data/register handoff
